shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: thymeleaf-spring6 3.1.2.RELEASE from spring-boot-starter-thymeleaf 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-40477)

fullDescription.text: Thymeleaf is a server-side Java template engine for web and standalone environments. Versions 3.1.3.RELEASE and prior contain a security bypass vulnerability in the expression execution mechanisms. Although the library provides mechanisms to prevent expression injection, it fails to properly restrict the scope of accessible objects, allowing specific potentially sensitive objects to be reached from within a template. If an application developer passes unvalidated user input directly to the template engine, an unauthenticated remote attacker can bypass the library's protections to achieve Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI). This issue has ben fixed in version 3.1.4.RELEASE.

## Description
Thymeleaf is a server-side Java template engine for web and standalone environments. Versions 3.1.3.RELEASE and prior contain a security bypass vulnerability in the expression execution mechanisms. Although the library provides mechanisms to prevent expression injection, it fails to properly restrict the scope of accessible objects, allowing specific potentially sensitive objects to be reached from within a template. If an application developer passes unvalidated user input directly to the template engine, an unauthenticated remote attacker can bypass the library's protections to achieve Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI). This issue has ben fixed in version 3.1.4.RELEASE.

## Description
Thymeleaf is vulnerable to server-side template injection (SSTI) due to improper input sanitization in the expression execution functionality. This could allow an attacker to bypass built-in template protections and achieve remote code execution (RCE).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-jdbc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

## Description
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

## Solution
Fixed in version [**6.2.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v6.2.11) and [**7.0.0-M9**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v7.0.0-M9) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/6d710d482a6785b069e35022e81758953afc21ff) and [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/0e3e34bee0c5b452b51c6ff6184e071a73ee2d40) commit.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-jdbc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-jdbc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-jdbc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22737 BDSA-2026-4873)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to information disclosure due to improper resource handling in `ScriptTemplateView` within Spring MVC and Spring WebFlux. This could allow an attacker to access content from files outside the configured locations for script template views.

**Note**: In order for the application to be vulnerable it must have a mapping for `/**` that results in view rendering where the view name is not explicitly specified.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-jdbc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

| **Affected Dependency**  | **Recommended short term version** | **Recommended long term version** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-jdbc 6.2.2  | 6.2.18  | 7.0.7 |

When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. ## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-jdbc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

## Description

A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-core 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41232 BDSA-2025-4341)

fullDescription.text:
An issue has been discovered in Spring Security resulting in the improper locating of method security annotations on private methods. An attacker could exploit this in order to achieve authorization bypass. 

Per the Vendor, applications may be affected by this if the following are true:
* Using @EnableMethodSecurity(mode=ASPECTJ) and spring-security-aspects, and
* Have Spring Security method annotations on a private method
	* If this is the case, the target method may be able to be invoked without proper authorization.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-core 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22223 BDSA-2025-2270)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to an authorization bypass issue due to how method security annotations on parameterized types and methods may not be correctly located in some circumstances.

For applications using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, method security annotations applied on a parameterized superclass, interface, or overridden method may not be interpreted correctly if no annotation is applied to the target method. This can allow for the target method to be invoked without sufficient authorization.

The vendor has noted that users are not affected if the following is true:
1. You are not using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, or
2. You do not have method security annotations on parameterized types or methods, or
3. All method security annotations are attached to target methods.

| :--- | :--- | :--- |

Fixed in [**6.4.4**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.4.4) and [**6.5.0-m3**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.5.0-M3) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/commit/dc2e1af2dab8ef81cd4edd25b56a2babeaab8cf9) commit.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-core 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22228 BDSA-2025-2271)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to a password authentication bypass issue due to how the `BCryptPasswordEncoder` functionality does not enforce a maximum password length limit.

The lack of a limit can result in authentication checks incorrectly permitting access. The `BCryptPasswordEncoder.matches(CharSequence,String)` function incorrectly returns `true` for passwords that are longer than 72 characters when the first 72 characters of the supplied password is the same.

**Note**: The original mitigation measures implemented for this flaw inadvertently reintroduced a timing attack issue. This issue was reported as **CVE-2025-22234** (**BDSA-2020-3483**). The **Solution** for **CVE-2025-22228** has been updated to account for this.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-core 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22748 BDSA-2026-7851)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is susceptible to a security misconfiguration issue when using `NimbusJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` or `NimbusReactiveJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` to configure JWT decoding.

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-security-core 6.4.2  | 6.5.10  | 7.0.5 |

## Description
Spring Security is susceptible to a security misconfiguration issue when using `NimbusJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` or `NimbusReactiveJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` to configure JWT decoding.

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-7851](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-7851)([CVE-2026-22748](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-22748)) - Improper Authentication


## Solution
Fixed in [**7.0.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/7.0.5) and in [**6.5.10**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.5.10) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/commit/68b820ed09d8d7d4b4170f1176c480b0a32f71d3) commit.

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases).

In the official [advisory](https://spring.io/security/cve-2026-22748), the vendor has provided the following advice regarding the fix for this issue:
> Note that if this upgrade causes you trouble due to unwanted issuer validation, you can change it to the earlier default in the following way:
> ```
> @Bean
> JwtDecoder jwtDecoder() {
>     String issuer = "https://issuer.example.org";
>     NimbusJwtDecoder jwtDecoder = NimbusJwtDecoder.withIssuerLocation(issuer)
>         // ... other configurations
>         .build();
>     jwtDecoder.setOAuth2TokenValidator(JwtValidators.createDefaults()); // set to the non-issuer default validator
>     return jwtDecoder;
> }
> ```

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-core 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22746 BDSA-2026-7849)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to an information disclosure issue when `DaoAuthenticationProvider` is in use as a result of how timing attack defenses can potentially be bypassed.

Specifically, where an application is using the `UserDetails#isEnabled`, `#isAccountNonExpired` or `#isAccountNonLocked` user attributes, defenses can be bypassed for users who are disabled, expired or locked. Authentication can take different lengths of time to complete depending on user account state, which leaks some user attributes.

Spring Security is vulnerable to an information disclosure issue when `DaoAuthenticationProvider` is in use as a result of how timing attack defenses can potentially be bypassed.

Specifically, where an application is using the `UserDetails#isEnabled`, `#isAccountNonExpired` or `#isAccountNonLocked` user attributes, defenses can be bypassed for users who are disabled, expired or locked. Authentication can take different lengths of time to complete depending on user account state, which leaks some user attributes.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-security-test 6.4.2 (CVE-2025-41248 BDSA-2025-11866)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-security-test 6.4.2 (CVE-2025-22223 BDSA-2025-2270)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to an authorization bypass issue due to how method security annotations on parameterized types and methods may not be correctly located in some circumstances.

For applications using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, method security annotations applied on a parameterized superclass, interface, or overridden method may not be interpreted correctly if no annotation is applied to the target method. This can allow for the target method to be invoked without sufficient authorization.

The vendor has noted that users are not affected if the following is true:
1. You are not using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, or
2. You do not have method security annotations on parameterized types or methods, or
3. All method security annotations are attached to target methods.

## Description
Spring Security is vulnerable to an authorization bypass issue due to how method security annotations on parameterized types and methods may not be correctly located in some circumstances.

For applications using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, method security annotations applied on a parameterized superclass, interface, or overridden method may not be interpreted correctly if no annotation is applied to the target method. This can allow for the target method to be invoked without sufficient authorization.

The vendor has noted that users are not affected if the following is true:
1. You are not using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, or
2. You do not have method security annotations on parameterized types or methods, or
3. All method security annotations are attached to target methods.

## Solution
Fixed in [**6.4.4**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.4.4) and [**6.5.0-m3**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.5.0-M3) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/commit/dc2e1af2dab8ef81cd4edd25b56a2babeaab8cf9) commit.

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases).

## Workaround
The vendor has provided the following advice in their [advisory](https://spring.io/security/cve-2025-22223) for users who cannot upgrade:
> If you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
> 1. Ensure the target method has the annotations instead of its parameterized ancestor, or
> 2. Publish an `AuthorizationManagerBeforeMethodInterceptor` that correctly looks for annotations on parameterized types (please see a sample in the Spring Security issue log)

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-security-test 6.4.2 (CVE-2025-22228 BDSA-2025-2271)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to a password authentication bypass issue due to how the `BCryptPasswordEncoder` functionality does not enforce a maximum password length limit.

The lack of a limit can result in authentication checks incorrectly permitting access. The `BCryptPasswordEncoder.matches(CharSequence,String)` function incorrectly returns `true` for passwords that are longer than 72 characters when the first 72 characters of the supplied password is the same.

**Note**: The original mitigation measures implemented for this flaw inadvertently reintroduced a timing attack issue. This issue was reported as **CVE-2025-22234** (**BDSA-2020-3483**). The **Solution** for **CVE-2025-22228** has been updated to account for this.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-security-test 6.4.2 (CVE-2026-22748 BDSA-2026-7851)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is susceptible to a security misconfiguration issue when using `NimbusJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` or `NimbusReactiveJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` to configure JWT decoding.

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

| :--- | :--- | :--- |

## Description
Spring Security is susceptible to a security misconfiguration issue when using `NimbusJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` or `NimbusReactiveJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` to configure JWT decoding.

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-7851](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-7851)([CVE-2026-22748](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-22748)) - Improper Authentication


## Solution
Fixed in [**7.0.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/7.0.5) and in [**6.5.10**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.5.10) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/commit/68b820ed09d8d7d4b4170f1176c480b0a32f71d3) commit.

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases).

In the official [advisory](https://spring.io/security/cve-2026-22748), the vendor has provided the following advice regarding the fix for this issue:
> Note that if this upgrade causes you trouble due to unwanted issuer validation, you can change it to the earlier default in the following way:
> ```
> @Bean
> JwtDecoder jwtDecoder() {
>     String issuer = "https://issuer.example.org";
>     NimbusJwtDecoder jwtDecoder = NimbusJwtDecoder.withIssuerLocation(issuer)
>         // ... other configurations
>         .build();
>     jwtDecoder.setOAuth2TokenValidator(JwtValidators.createDefaults()); // set to the non-issuer default validator
>     return jwtDecoder;
> }
> ```

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-security-test 6.4.2 (CVE-2026-22746 BDSA-2026-7849)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to an information disclosure issue when `DaoAuthenticationProvider` is in use as a result of how timing attack defenses can potentially be bypassed.

Specifically, where an application is using the `UserDetails#isEnabled`, `#isAccountNonExpired` or `#isAccountNonLocked` user attributes, defenses can be bypassed for users who are disabled, expired or locked. Authentication can take different lengths of time to complete depending on user account state, which leaks some user attributes.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-tx 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-tx 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-tx 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-tx 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

help.markdown:

| **Affected Dependency**  | **Recommended short term version** | **Recommended long term version** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-tx 6.2.2  | 6.2.18  | 7.0.7 |

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-tx 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

help.markdown:

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

Fixed in [**6.2.18**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v6.2.18) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/684b1e8a4b3df9589f3e9abb8c09a5e98a47e4b5) commit and in [**7.0.7**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v7.0.7) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/e977bd7bf0efe8341a03de025ee6ac376e6a57c8) commit.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-tx 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-actuator 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-actuator 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

fullDescription.text:

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: assertj-core 3.26.3 from spring-boot-starter-test 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-24400 BDSA-2026-1145)

fullDescription.text:
AssertJ is vulnerable to XML external entity (XXE) injection as the `DocumentBuilderFactory` class is initialized with an insecure default configuration in the `XmlStringPrettyFormatter.java` file. A local attacker could exploit this issue by using a maliciously crafted XML input in order to read the content of arbitrary files on the local file system, perform server-side request forgery (SSRF) or create a denial-of-service (DoS) condition.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that this vulnerability can only be exploited if untrusted XML input is passed to the `isXmlEqualTo`or `xmlPrettyFormat` methods.

## Description
AssertJ is vulnerable to XML external entity (XXE) injection as the `DocumentBuilderFactory` class is initialized with an insecure default configuration in the `XmlStringPrettyFormatter.java` file. A local attacker could exploit this issue by using a maliciously crafted XML input in order to read the content of arbitrary files on the local file system, perform server-side request forgery (SSRF) or create a denial-of-service (DoS) condition.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that this vulnerability can only be exploited if untrusted XML input is passed to the `isXmlEqualTo`or `xmlPrettyFormat` methods.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-1145](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-1145)([CVE-2026-24400](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-24400)) - XML External Entity (XXE) Injection


## Solution
Fixed in version [**3.27.7**](https://github.com/assertj/assertj/releases/tag/assertj-build-3.27.7) by [this](https://github.com/assertj/assertj/commit/85ca7eb6609bb179c043b85ae7d290523b1ba79a) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/assertj/assertj/releases).

## Workaround
The vendor has provided the following workarounds [here](https://github.com/assertj/assertj/security/advisories/GHSA-rqfh-9r24-8c9r):
* Replace `isXmlEqualTo(CharSequence)` with `XMLUnit`
* Avoid using `isXmlEqualTo(CharSequence)` or `XmlStringPrettyFormatter` with untrusted input.

## Description
jose4j is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to improper handling of compressed content. An unauthenticated remote attacker could exploit this by supplying a crafted JSON web encryption (JWE) token with an excessively high compression ratio. Successful exploitation could allow for a crash in the application.

## Solution

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://bitbucket.org/b_c/jose4j/downloads/?tab=tags).

## Workaround
The vendor suggests the following workaround:
> To mitigate this vulnerability, it is recommended to limit the maximum token length to 250K.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

help.markdown:
## Upgrade Guidance



| **Affected Dependency**  | **Recommended short term version** | **Recommended long term version** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2  | 3.5.14  | 4.0.6 |


[Click Here To See More Details in Polaris](https://poc.polaris.blackduck.com/portfolio/portfolios/00296cd0-45bc-40be-8d02-fc1a3203abde/portfolio-items/756b9ef1-3563-4c3e-9777-11535f3c826c/projects/8b1eac8c-2938-4078-bba6-eb01275e08a4/components/5d832af9-0fa9-418b-b365-a4e137a25fb2?branchId=2c3e2c1f-0e35-4f81-ab88-73d0ab4f14a4&component=ee1b331d-05e0-4bd1-b6ef-727c3b98e324)


## Description

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22235 BDSA-2025-3548)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-context 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-context 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-context 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-context 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

help.markdown:

| **Affected Dependency**  | **Recommended short term version** | **Recommended long term version** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-context 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-context 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

## Description
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

Successful exploitation could allow for unauthorized access or manipulation of clustered communication. 


**Note:** This issue occurs due to an incomplete fix for **CVE-2026-29146** (**BDSA-2026-6723**).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: tomcat-embed-websocket 10.1.34 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-24813 BDSA-2025-1980)

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to remote code execution (RCE), information disclosure, or corruption of information via a write-enabled Default Servlet. The impact of exploitation depends on the following: 

If all of the following were true, a malicious user was able to view security sensitive files and/or inject content into those files: 
* writes enabled for the default servlet (disabled by default)
* support for partial PUT (enabled by default)
* a target URL for security sensitive uploads that was a sub-directory of a target URL for public uploads
* attacker knowledge of the names of security sensitive files being uploaded
* the security sensitive files also being uploaded via partial PUT

If all of the following were true, a malicious user was able to perform remote code execution:
* writes enabled for the default servlet (disabled by default)
* support for partial PUT (enabled by default)
* application was using Tomcat's file based session persistence with the default storage location
* application included a library that may be leveraged in a deserialization attack

This vulnerability is listed as exploitable by the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency in their [Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog](https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: tomcat-embed-websocket 10.1.34 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-29146 BDSA-2026-6723)

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to a padding oracle attack due to the default use of `CBC` mode with `PKCS5` padding in the `EncryptInterceptor` component. This could allow a remote attacker to progressively decrypt or manipulate encrypted cluster messages by observing differences in error handling during decryption attempts, potentially exposing or allowing for the modification of sensitive data within the cluster.


**Note:** The original fix for this vulnerability has proven to be insufficient and has been assigned **CVE-2026-34486** (**BDSA-2026-6784**).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: tomcat-embed-websocket 10.1.34 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-55752 BDSA-2025-14737)

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to path traversal due to improper URL normalization in the `RewriteValve` component which can allow for the bypassing of security constraints. This could be leveraged by an attacker to potentially execute malicious code against deployments using URL-manipulating rewrites and PUT requests are enabled.

**Note**: The vendor states that: "PUT requests are normally limited to trusted users and it is considered unlikely that PUT requests would be enabled in conjunction with a rewrite that manipulated the URI."

Apache Tomcat fails to properly handle security restraints of the `pathInfo` component of a URL that maps to the CGI servlet on case insensitive filesystems. A remote attacker could exploit this to bypass security restrictions, which could be leveraged if a servlet uses the `pathInfo` to identify other resources.

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) issue due to the presence of an uncontrolled resource consumption flaw affecting the HTTP/2 connection functionality.

A malicious HTTP/2 client could abuse this issue by ignoring the initial settings frame that reduces the maximum permitted concurrent streams and sending an excessive amount of streams to an affected server to induce the excessive resource consumption conditions, negatively impacting performance.

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to certificate verification bypass due to improper virtual host mapping in applications enforcing client certificate authentication at the Connector. An attacker could exploit this by sending different host names in the SNI extension and the HTTP host header field. Successful exploitation could allow for a client to bypass the client certificate authentication. 

**Note:** The vulnerability does not apply in settings where client certificate authentication is enforced at the web application.

**Note:** The original fix for this vulnerability was insufficient and could be bypassed. This issue was assigned **CVE-2026-32990** (**BDSA-2026-6768**).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: tomcat-embed-websocket 10.1.34 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-31651 BDSA-2025-3616)

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to a rewrite rule bypass issue due to how some literal characters are handled.

An attacker could abuse this issue by submitting a specially crafted request that includes unexpected characters in the request URL in order to bypass rewrite rules. This issue could potentially allow security constraints to be bypassed if the rewrite rules were being used to enforce such constraints.

The vendor has noted in their official [advisory](https://lists.apache.org/thread/cpklvqwvdrp4k9hmd2l3q33j0gzy4fox) that only a subset of unlikely rewrite rule configurations are impacted.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: tomcat-embed-websocket 10.1.34 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-31650 BDSA-2025-3615)

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) issue due to how errors are not correctly handled during processing of invalid HTTP priority headers.

The incorrect error handling can cause an incomplete clean-up of failed requests and lead to a memory leak. An attacker could abuse this issue by submitting a large number of these requests and trigger an `OutOfMemoryException`, causing a crash.

**Note**: This issue only affects use of HTTP/2 (H2) and HTTP/2 Cleartext (H2C).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: tomcat-embed-websocket 10.1.34 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-34483 BDSA-2026-6774)

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to JSON access log injection due to incomplete escaping of request paths and query strings in the `AbstractAccessLogValve.java` file. This issue could allow a remote attacker to inject arbitrary JSON fragments into access log entries, compromising the integrity of log data. This could result in cross-site scripting or other problems in downstream parsing/display components.

**Note:** This requires the application to use non-default values for the `Connector` attributes `relaxedPathChars` and/or `relaxedQueryChars`.

Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to JSON access log injection due to incomplete escaping of request paths and query strings in the `AbstractAccessLogValve.java` file. This issue could allow a remote attacker to inject arbitrary JSON fragments into access log entries, compromising the integrity of log data. This could result in cross-site scripting or other problems in downstream parsing/display components.

**Note:** This requires the application to use non-default values for the `Connector` attributes `relaxedPathChars` and/or `relaxedQueryChars`.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-6774](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-6774)([CVE-2026-34483](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-34483)) - Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output


## Solution
Fixed in:
* [**11.0.21**](https://github.com/apache/tomcat/releases/tag/11.0.21) by [this](https://github.com/apache/tomcat/commit/f9ddc24fcfcdfaea4a6953198d8636aca3e957bc) commit.
* [**10.1.54**](https://github.com/apache/tomcat/releases/tag/10.1.54) by [this](https://github.com/apache/tomcat/commit/f22dc2ce6cfda8609ed86816c0d78e1a9cbadb06) commit.
* [**9.0.117**](https://github.com/apache/tomcat/releases/tag/9.0.117) by [this](https://github.com/apache/tomcat/commit/97566842589d0b80de138ca719378861fd017d68) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/apache/tomcat/tags).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: tomcat-embed-websocket 10.1.34 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-61795 BDSA-2025-14740)

fullDescription.text:
Apache Tomcat is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) issue due to how cleanup operations of temporary files can be delayed under certain circumstances.

An attacker could upload crafted multipart upload content that causes an error to occur during processing of the upload. Doing so could then cause excessive space to be consumed by temporary copies of uploaded parts that are not yet cleaned up by the JVM garbage collection process. Depending on settings, application memory usage and load, it could be possible for the space to be filled faster than it is cleared, leading to a denial-of-service (DoS).

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aop 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-security 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

## Description
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aop 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-security 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aop 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-security 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to information disclosure due to improper resource handling in `ScriptTemplateView` within Spring MVC and Spring WebFlux. This could allow an attacker to access content from files outside the configured locations for script template views.

**Note**: In order for the application to be vulnerable it must have a mapping for `/**` that results in view rendering where the view name is not explicitly specified.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aop 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-security 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aop 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-security 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aop 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-security 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

## Description
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

## Description
Spring Security is vulnerable to improper handling of HTTP response headers due to missing overrides in the `OnCommittedResponseWrapper` component. This could allow an attacker to cause the response body to be committed before the response headers are written, potentially leading to inconsistent or incomplete HTTP responses.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-crypto 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22223 BDSA-2025-2270)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to an authorization bypass issue due to how method security annotations on parameterized types and methods may not be correctly located in some circumstances.

For applications using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, method security annotations applied on a parameterized superclass, interface, or overridden method may not be interpreted correctly if no annotation is applied to the target method. This can allow for the target method to be invoked without sufficient authorization.

The vendor has noted that users are not affected if the following is true:
1. You are not using `@EnableMethodSecurity`, or
2. You do not have method security annotations on parameterized types or methods, or
3. All method security annotations are attached to target methods.

Fixed in [**6.4.4**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.4.4) and [**6.5.0-m3**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.5.0-M3) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/commit/dc2e1af2dab8ef81cd4edd25b56a2babeaab8cf9) commit.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-crypto 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22228 BDSA-2025-2271)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to a password authentication bypass issue due to how the `BCryptPasswordEncoder` functionality does not enforce a maximum password length limit.

The lack of a limit can result in authentication checks incorrectly permitting access. The `BCryptPasswordEncoder.matches(CharSequence,String)` function incorrectly returns `true` for passwords that are longer than 72 characters when the first 72 characters of the supplied password is the same.

**Note**: The original mitigation measures implemented for this flaw inadvertently reintroduced a timing attack issue. This issue was reported as **CVE-2025-22234** (**BDSA-2020-3483**). The **Solution** for **CVE-2025-22228** has been updated to account for this.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-crypto 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22748 BDSA-2026-7851)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is susceptible to a security misconfiguration issue when using `NimbusJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` or `NimbusReactiveJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` to configure JWT decoding.

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

## Description
Spring Security is susceptible to a security misconfiguration issue when using `NimbusJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` or `NimbusReactiveJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` to configure JWT decoding.

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-7851](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-7851)([CVE-2026-22748](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-22748)) - Improper Authentication


## Solution
Fixed in [**7.0.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/7.0.5) and in [**6.5.10**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases/tag/6.5.10) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/commit/68b820ed09d8d7d4b4170f1176c480b0a32f71d3) commit.

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-security/releases).

In the official [advisory](https://spring.io/security/cve-2026-22748), the vendor has provided the following advice regarding the fix for this issue:
> Note that if this upgrade causes you trouble due to unwanted issuer validation, you can change it to the earlier default in the following way:
> ```
> @Bean
> JwtDecoder jwtDecoder() {
>     String issuer = "https://issuer.example.org";
>     NimbusJwtDecoder jwtDecoder = NimbusJwtDecoder.withIssuerLocation(issuer)
>         // ... other configurations
>         .build();
>     jwtDecoder.setOAuth2TokenValidator(JwtValidators.createDefaults()); // set to the non-issuer default validator
>     return jwtDecoder;
> }
> ```





## Dependency tree

    - org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client:3.4.2
        - org.springframework.security:spring-security-core:6.4.2
            - org.springframework.security:spring-security-crypto:6.4.2


## References
* Common Weakness Enumeration: [CWE-287](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html)
* Common Weakness Enumeration: [CWE-345](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/345.html)
help.text:
Spring Security is susceptible to a security misconfiguration issue when using `NimbusJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` or `NimbusReactiveJwtDecoder#withIssuerLocation` to configure JWT decoding.

By default, the decoder only verifies the cryptographic signature of JWTs without validating the issuer, unless developers explicitly add an `OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt>` by calling `setJwtValidator()`. This creates a dangerous security gap where applications may unknowingly accept JWTs from any source as long as they are properly signed.

This default state could lead to authentication bypass scenarios where an attacker sends fraudulent tokens to an affected application wherein they are accepted because the application only checks if the signature is valid, not whether the token came from the trusted authorization server.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-security-crypto 6.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22746 BDSA-2026-7849)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Security is vulnerable to an information disclosure issue when `DaoAuthenticationProvider` is in use as a result of how timing attack defenses can potentially be bypassed.

Specifically, where an application is using the `UserDetails#isEnabled`, `#isAccountNonExpired` or `#isAccountNonLocked` user attributes, defenses can be bypassed for users who are disabled, expired or locked. Authentication can take different lengths of time to complete depending on user account state, which leaks some user attributes.

## Description
XStream is vulnerable to unsafe deserialization of attacker controlled input. A remote attacker could leverage this to execute arbitrary system commands.

Note that if the application uses XStream's security framework and allowlist approach to deserialization (recommended), it is not vulnerable.

This vulnerability is listed as exploitable by the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency in their [Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog](https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog).

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: xstream 1.4.5 (CVE-2021-21347 BDSA-2021-0722)

fullDescription.text:
XStream, when in its default configuration or operating without a sufficient blacklist / whitelist configuration, is vulnerable to an arbitrary code execution issue. This is due to how XStream can potentially create new instances of dangerous objects that are not blocked based on the contents of the input stream. An attacker could supply a crafted input to XStream in order execute arbitrary code stored on a remote host.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: xstream 1.4.5 (CVE-2016-3674 BDSA-2016-0027)

fullDescription.text:
XStream, when using some supported parsers, is vulnerable to an XML entity expansion issue due to how these parsers can process external entities by default.

A remote attacker could use this issue to retrieve the content of arbitrary files with known locations in a local file system if the Java process has read access.

**Note**: XStream is not vulnerable if the default XML Pull Parser (`Xpp3` or `kXML2`) is used. These parser types do not process XML entities.

## Description
XStream, when using some supported parsers, is vulnerable to an XML entity expansion issue due to how these parsers can process external entities by default.

A remote attacker could use this issue to retrieve the content of arbitrary files with known locations in a local file system if the Java process has read access.

**Note**: XStream is not vulnerable if the default XML Pull Parser (`Xpp3` or `kXML2`) is used. These parser types do not process XML entities.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2016-0027](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2016-0027)([CVE-2016-3674](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2016-3674)) - XML External Entity (XXE) Injection


## Solution
This vulnerability is fixed in version [**1.4.9**](http://repo.maven.apache.org/maven2/com/thoughtworks/xstream/xstream/1.4.9/).

**Note**: Applications using one of the following parser implementations are still vulnerable:
* `DOM` implementation from Java 5 runtime and below
* `StAX` implementation from Java 6 runtime and below
* `StAX` implementation from BEA (old reference implementation)
* `XOM`

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://x-stream.github.io/download.html).

## Workaround
The vendor has recommended [here](https://x-stream.github.io/CVE-2016-3674.html) that users can opt to use one of the XML Pull Parser implementations in order to mitigate this issue.





## References
* Common Weakness Enumeration: [CWE-611](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/611.html)
* Common Weakness Enumeration: [CWE-776](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/776.html)

**Note**: XStream is not vulnerable if the default XML Pull Parser (`Xpp3` or `kXML2`) is used. These parser types do not process XML entities.

This is due to how XStream can potentially create new instances of dangerous objects that are not blocked based on the contents of the input stream. The potentially dangerous type in this case is `com.sun.corba.se.impl.activation.ServerTableEntry`.

An attacker could supply a crafted input to XStream in order execute arbitrary commands on the XStream host.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: xstream 1.4.5 (CVE-2021-21341 BDSA-2021-0737)

fullDescription.text:
XStream, when in its default configuration or operating without a sufficient blacklist / whitelist configuration, is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) issue. This is due to how XStream can potentially create new instances of dangerous objects that are not blocked based on the contents of the input stream. The potentially dangerous type in this case is `java.io.ByteArrayInputStream`.

An attacker could supply a crafted input to XStream in order to cause an endless loop, negatively impacting the availability of XStream.
