## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2022-0291](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2022-0291)([CVE-2021-43859](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2021-43859)) - Uncontrolled Resource Consumption


## Solution
Fixed in [**1.4.19**](https://github.com/x-stream/xstream/releases/tag/XSTREAM_1_4_19) by [this](https://github.com/x-stream/xstream/commit/e8e88621ba1c85ac3b8620337dd672e0c0c3a846) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/x-stream/xstream/tags).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor [workaround](https://x-stream.github.io/CVE-2021-43859.html):

> If your object graph does not use referenced elements at all, you may simply set the NO_REFERENCE mode:
> ```
> XStream xstream = new XStream();
> xstream.setMode(XStream.NO_REFERENCES);
> ```
> If your object graph contains neither a Hashtable, HashMap nor a HashSet (or one of the linked variants of it) then you can use the security framework to deny the usage of these types:
> ```
> XStream xstream = new XStream();
> xstream.denyTypes(new Class[]{
> 	java.util.HashMap.class, java.util.HashSet.class, java.util.Hashtable.class, java.util.LinkedHashMap.class, java.util.LinkedHashSet.class
> });
> ```
> 
> Unfortunately these types are very common. If you only use HashMap or HashSet and your XML refers these only as default map or set, you may additionally change the default implementation of java.util.Map and java.util.Set at unmarshalling time:
> 
> ```
> xstream.addDefaultImplementation(java.util.TreeMap.class, java.util.Map.class);
> xstream.addDefaultImplementation(java.util.TreeSet.class, java.util.Set.class);
> ```
> However, this implies that your application does not care about the implementation of the map and all elements are comparable.

## Description
XStream is vulnerable to denial-of-service due to a stack overflow that exists due to a recursive hash set calculation. A remote attacker could cause a vulnerable application to crash by causing that application to process a maliciously crafted input stream.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2022-3693](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2022-3693)([CVE-2022-41966](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2022-41966)) - Insecure Object Deserialization


## Solution
Fixed in version [**1.4.20**](https://github.com/x-stream/xstream/releases/tag/XSTREAM_1_4_20) by [this](https://github.com/x-stream/xstream/commit/e9151f221b4969fb15b1e946d5d61dcdd459a391) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/x-stream/xstream/tags).

## Workaround
The vendor has stated [here](https://github.com/x-stream/xstream/security/advisories/GHSA-j563-grx4-pjpv) that the following workaround can be implemented:

> The attack uses the hash code implementation for collections and maps to force recursive hash calculation causing a stack overflow. Following types of the Java runtime are affected:
>
> * java.util.HashMap
> * java.util.HashSet
> * java.util.Hashtable
> * java.util.LinkedHashMap
> * java.util.LinkedHashSet
> Other third party collection implementations that use their element's hash code may also be affected
> A simple solution is to catch the StackOverflowError in the client code calling XStream.
> 
> If your object graph does not use referenced elements at all, you may simply set the NO_REFERENCE mode:
> 
> XStream xstream = new XStream();
> xstream.setMode(XStream.NO_REFERENCES);
> If your object graph contains neither a Hashtable, HashMap nor a HashSet (or one of the linked variants of it) then you can use the security framework to deny the usage of these types:
> 
> XStream xstream = new XStream();
> xstream.denyTypes(new Class[]{
>  java.util.HashMap.class, java.util.HashSet.class, java.util.Hashtable.class, java.util.LinkedHashMap.class, java.util.LinkedHashSet.class
> });
> Unfortunately these types are very common. If you only use HashMap or HashSet and your XML refers these only as default map or set, you may additionally change the default implementation of java.util.Map and java.util.Set at unmarshalling time::
> 
> xstream.addDefaultImplementation(java.util.TreeMap.class, java.util.Map.class);
> xstream.addDefaultImplementation(java.util.TreeSet.class, java.util.Set.class);
> However, this implies that your application does not care about the implementation of the map and all elements are comparable.

The vendor recommends the use of XStream's security framework to implement a whitelist for allowed types. 

The vendor has made available a set of [workarounds](https://x-stream.github.io/security.html#workaround) detailing this feature.





## References
* Common Weakness Enumeration: [CWE-502](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/502.html)
help.text:
XStream, when in its default configuration or operating without a sufficient blacklist / whitelist configuration, is vulnerable to an arbitrary file deletion issue. This is due to how XStream can potentially create new instances of dangerous objects that are not blocked based on the contents of the input stream. The potentially dangerous types in this case are  `sun.awt.datatransfer.DataTransferer$IndexOrderComparator` and `java.io.FileInputStream`.

An attacker could supply a crafted input to XStream in order deletion arbitrary files on the local host.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-beans 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-beans 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-beans 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

help.markdown:

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-beans 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

help.markdown:
## Upgrade Guidance

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

Fixed in [**6.2.18**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v6.2.18) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/684b1e8a4b3df9589f3e9abb8c09a5e98a47e4b5) commit and in [**7.0.7**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v7.0.7) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/e977bd7bf0efe8341a03de025ee6ac376e6a57c8) commit.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-beans 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22235 BDSA-2025-3548)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter-tomcat 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter-tomcat 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aspects 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aspects 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aspects 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aspects 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-aspects 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-expression 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

## Description
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. **Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-expression 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-expression 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-expression 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-expression 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-expression 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-web 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-web 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-web 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-web 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-web 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-web 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

## Description
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-autoconfigure 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

help.markdown:

| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-boot-autoconfigure 3.4.2  | 3.5.14  | 4.0.6 |

This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-autoconfigure 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-autoconfigure 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22235 BDSA-2025-3548)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

help.markdown:

| **Affected Dependency**  | **Recommended short term version** | **Recommended long term version** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2  | 3.5.14  | 4.0.6 |

This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22235 BDSA-2025-3548)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

| :--- | :--- | :--- |

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-thymeleaf 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-boot-starter-thymeleaf 3.4.2  | 3.5.14  | 4.0.6 |

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-thymeleaf 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Direct Dependency: spring-boot-starter-thymeleaf 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22235 BDSA-2025-3548)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22235 BDSA-2025-3548)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-webmvc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-webmvc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-webmvc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-webmvc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22735 BDSA-2026-4886)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework MVC and WebFlux are vulnerable to stream corruption due to improper handling of server-sent events (SSE).

An application is vulnerable when using Spring MVC's `SseEmitter` or Spring WebFlux's `ServerSentEvent` to send Server-Sent Events (SSE) to clients, and an attacker has control over values used in the SSE id or event fields. By injecting newline characters (`\n`, `\r`, or `\r\n`) into these fields, an attacker can break out of the intended SSE field and inject arbitrary SSE events (such as malicious data:, event:, or id: fields) into the stream. Plain text strings in the data field containing CR/CRLF characters are vulnerable but JSON data would be safe in the datafield as the JSON parser would would escape all newline variants properly.
This could allow the attacker to corrupt the SSE stream received by other users, potentially altering application state or presenting false information to victims.

help.markdown:

| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| spring-webmvc 6.2.2  | 6.2.18  | 7.0.7 |

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-webmvc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7607)

fullDescription.text: Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

help.markdown:
## Upgrade Guidance



| **Affected Dependency**  | **Recommended short term version** | **Recommended long term version** |

| spring-webmvc 6.2.2  | 6.2.18  | 7.0.7 |

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing existence check in the `FileSystemResource.isReadable()` method used during static resource resolution. When serving static resources from the file system on Windows, the `isReadable()` method called `File.canRead()` without first verifying that the file exists. On Windows, `canRead()` performs expensive security descriptor lookups even for non-existent files, causing these calls to be slow to return. This could allow an attacker to send a high volume of crafted requests for non-existent static resources, keeping HTTP connections occupied and eventually exhausting available connections, resulting in a denial-of-service condition for legitimate users.

Fixed in [**6.2.18**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v6.2.18) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/684b1e8a4b3df9589f3e9abb8c09a5e98a47e4b5) commit and in [**7.0.7**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/releases/tag/v7.0.7) by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-framework/commit/e977bd7bf0efe8341a03de025ee6ac376e6a57c8) commit.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-webmvc 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-web 3.4.2 (BDSA-2026-7608)

fullDescription.text:
Spring framework contains a cache poisoning vulnerability due to how static resources are resolved when using Spring MVD or Spring Webflux. A remote attacker could exploit this flaw by tricking a victim into interacting with poisoned content, which could lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) and impacts to application performance.

To be vulnerable to this flaw, an application must meet the following conditions:

* the application is using Spring MVC or Spring WebFlux,
* the application is configuring the resource chain support with caching enabled,
* the application adds support for encoded resources resolution,
* the resource cache must be empty when the attacker has access to the application.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter-logging 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter-logging 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-validation 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22733 BDSA-2026-4925)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter-jdbc 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2026-22731 BDSA-2026-4914)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of endpoint request matchers in the `EndpointRequest` component. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication, potentially exposing sensitive functionality or data.

An application is only vulnerable when it combines Spring Boot Actuator with a specific configuration pattern: the application must have the Actuator dependency on its classpath and configure a custom health group (e.g., "mygroup") using properties like `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.include`. This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). In this scenario, the flawed path matching logic causes the health group matcher to incorrectly match the authentication-protected endpoint, potentially allowing unauthenticated access to what should be a secured resource.

This health group must then be exposed under an additional path on the main server using a configuration such as `management.endpoint.health.group.mygroup.additional-path=server:/healthz`. The vulnerability becomes exploitable when the application also defines an endpoint requiring authentication that is located under a subpath of this additional health path (e.g., `/healthz/admin`). help.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to authentication bypass due to improper handling of requests in the `CloudFoundry` actuator endpoint mapping. This could allow an attacker to access protected application endpoints without proper authentication.

**Note:** A Spring Boot application is only vulnerable when both the Actuator and Spring Security dependencies are on the classpath. The application must have a custom endpoint that requires authentication mapped under the `CloudFoundry` actuator path (such as `/cloudfoundryapplication/admin`). This specific misconfiguration of placing application endpoints under the infrastructure actuator namespace is the key prerequisite for this vulnerability to manifest.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-boot-starter-jdbc 3.4.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-22235 BDSA-2025-3548)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Description
Spring Boot is vulnerable to creation of an improper matcher for `null/**` in cases where the associated `EndpointRequest` is disabled, or not exposed.

**Note:** Applications may be affected by this if all the following conditions are met:
* `EndpointRequest.to()` has been used in a Spring Security chain configuration
* The endpoint which `EndpointRequest` references is disabled or not exposed via web
* Your application handles requests to `/null` and this path needs protection

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-3548](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-3548)([CVE-2025-22235](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-22235)) - Improper Null Termination


## Solution
Fixed by [this](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/commit/55f67c9a522647039fd3294dee5cb83f4888160a) commit in [**3.4.5**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.4.5) and in [**3.3.11**](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases/tag/v3.3.11). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot/releases).

## Workaround
Per the Vendor, if you cannot upgrade, then you can either:
* Make sure that the endpoint to which `EndpointRequest.to()` is referring to is enabled and exposed via web
* Make sure that you don't handle requests to `/null`

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-orm 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41249 BDSA-2025-11864)

fullDescription.text:
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

## Description
The Spring Framework might not correctly detect annotations on methods if those methods are part of a class hierarchy that uses generic types without specific type limits. If these annotations are used for authorization purposes, a remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to reveal information that they should not have access to.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that only instances of spring that use the `@EnableMethodSecurity` setting or use security annotations on methods in generic superclasses or generic interfaces are vulnerable.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-orm 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41234 BDSA-2025-5150)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

## Description
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

help.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to a reflected file download (RFD) attack when a `Content-Disposition` header is set with a non-ASCII charset.

Specifically, an application is vulnerable when all the following are true:
* The header is prepared with `org.springframework.http.ContentDisposition`.
* The filename is set via `ContentDisposition.Builder#filename(String, Charset)`.
* The value for the filename is derived from user-supplied input.
* The application does not sanitize the user-supplied input.
* The downloaded content of the response is injected with malicious commands by the attacker.

shortDescription.text: Transitive Dependency: spring-orm 6.2.2 from spring-boot-starter-data-jpa 3.4.2 (CVE-2025-41242 BDSA-2025-8611)

fullDescription.text:
Spring Framework is vulnerable to path traversal when it has been deployed on a non-compliant servlet container. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability in order to view sensitive information.

An application can be vulnerable when all the following are true:
* the application is deployed as a WAR or with an embedded Servlet container,
* the Servlet container does not reject suspicious sequences,
* the application serves static resources with Spring resource handling.

**Note**: The vendor has stated that applications deployed on Apache Tomcat or Eclipse Jetty are not vulnerable, as long as default security features are not disabled in the configuration.
